Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-2-0-CONF-CONV-001.businessIssue: Your tracking system is counting page visits as conversions. This inflates your real conversion numbers and leads to wrong decisions about ad budget.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-2-0-CONF-CONV-001.businessDetail: Every time someone opens a page on your site, it's being counted as a success metric. This makes your conversion rate look much higher than it really is, distorts your revenue-per-visit numbers, and causes Google Ads and Meta to optimize toward the wrong goal. You end up wasting ad budget chasing page views instead of real purchases.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-2-0-CONF-CONV-001.businessFix:
1. Open Google Analytics → Admin → Settings → Key events.
2. Find 'Page view' in the list and turn off the key event toggle.
3. Keep 'Page view' as a normal event so you can still see traffic, but remove it as a conversion metric.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-2-0-CONF-CONV-001.businessVerify: After this change, your conversion count in Google Analytics will drop to match your real purchases, and your ad platforms will optimize toward actual sales instead of page visits.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-3-0-CONF-CONV-002.businessIssue: Your tracking system is counting every new visitor as a conversion. This inflates your conversion numbers.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-3-0-CONF-CONV-002.businessDetail: A session start only means someone opened your site—it's not a purchase or engagement. By marking it as a conversion, you're artificially inflating your conversion metrics and misleading your ad platforms about what success looks like. This causes wasted ad budget on poor targeting and wrong optimization.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-3-0-CONF-CONV-002.businessFix:
1. Open Google Analytics → Admin → Settings → Key events.
2. Find 'Session start' in the list and turn off the key event toggle.
3. Keep tracking it as a normal metric so you can monitor traffic, but stop counting it as a conversion.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-3-0-CONF-CONV-002.businessVerify: Your conversion metrics will drop to reflect only real business actions, and your ad platforms will optimize correctly toward actual purchases.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-7-0-CONF-CUST-004.businessIssue: Product categories are missing from your tracking data. You cannot see which types of products are driving sales.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-7-0-CONF-CUST-004.businessDetail: Without product category data, you cannot answer: Which product lines are most profitable? Which categories have the highest return rate? How are different segments performing? This blind spot makes it impossible to optimize inventory, pricing, and marketing by product type.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-7-0-CONF-CUST-004.businessFix:
1. Check your Shopify Admin → Products and verify each product has a 'Product type' or 'Collection' assigned.
2. If products are missing categories, bulk-edit them in Shopify to add the correct category.
3. Ask your developer to verify that Google Tag Manager is sending product category data with each product event.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-7-0-CONF-CUST-004.businessVerify: After this is fixed, you'll see product category breakdowns in your Google Analytics reports and understand which product lines drive the most revenue.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-8-0-CONF-CUST-008.businessIssue: You're missing context about which page or collection each product view came from. All product activity is grouped together.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-8-0-CONF-CUST-008.businessDetail: Without this context, you cannot identify which product lists or collections are driving the most sales, which recommendations work best, or how to optimize your homepage layout. You're flying blind when it comes to understanding which browsing paths convert best. This prevents you from improving product discovery and increasing average order value.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-8-0-CONF-CUST-008.businessFix:
1. Ask your developer to add product-list tracking to your Google Tag Manager setup.
2. When a product is viewed from a collection, search results, or homepage, the tracking should record the source.
3. Test the tracking by browsing your site and confirming product list names appear in Google Analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-8-0-CONF-CUST-008.businessVerify: Once working, you'll see which collections and product lists drive the most conversions, allowing you to optimize your store layout and merchandising.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-10-0-CONF-PRIV-005.businessIssue: Your site is tracking all visitors without asking for permission first. This creates a privacy and legal risk in the EU, UK, and other regulated regions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-10-0-CONF-PRIV-005.businessDetail: Currently, every visitor is tracked for analytics, ads, and data selling before they consent. This violates EU GDPR and UK data protection laws, which require explicit consent before tracking. You could face regulatory fines, customer lawsuits, or forced shutdown in those regions. Even if your current visitors aren't from regulated areas, browsers are increasingly blocking non-consenting tracking.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-10-0-CONF-PRIV-005.businessFix:
1. Install a consent banner tool (Cookiebot, OneTrust, or Pandectes for Shopify).
2. Configure it to ask visitors for consent before analytics or ads tracking starts.
3. Set the default consent state to 'denied' and only enable tracking after explicit user agreement.
4. Ask your developer to integrate the banner with your Google Tag Manager setup.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-10-0-CONF-PRIV-005.businessVerify: You'll see a consent banner when you visit your site. Visitors can choose to accept or decline tracking, and only those who consent will be tracked.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-11-0-CONF-PRIV-007.businessIssue: Your consent settings are locked at startup and never respond to visitor choices. Even if a visitor declines tracking, you keep tracking anyway.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-11-0-CONF-PRIV-007.businessDetail: This is a critical compliance failure. Visitors who click 'decline' on your consent banner are still being tracked because your system never receives or respects their choice. This violates privacy laws and erodes customer trust. You could face regulatory fines and are exposed to automated cookie compliance lawsuits.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-11-0-CONF-PRIV-007.businessFix:
1. Install a consent banner tool (Cookiebot, OneTrust, or Pandectes).
2. Configure it to store the visitor's choice in a cookie.
3. Ask your developer to read that cookie on page load and update the tracking consent based on the stored choice.
4. Test by declining consent, leaving the site, and returning to verify tracking respects your saved preference.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-11-0-CONF-PRIV-007.businessVerify: When you change your consent preference, your tracking state updates immediately. Declining consent stops analytics and ad tracking; accepting re-enables them.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-12-0-CONF-PRIV-008-.businessIssue: All tracking consent is permanently set to 'yes' without asking visitors. This violates privacy laws in the EU, UK, and other regions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-12-0-CONF-PRIV-008-.businessDetail: Your site is collecting analytics data, setting ad tracking cookies, storing user data for ad targeting, and enabling personalized ads—all without visitor consent. This violates GDPR (EU), Data Protection Act (UK), and similar laws. Penalties can exceed 4% of annual revenue, and you're exposed to customer lawsuits and forced blocking in regulated regions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-12-0-CONF-PRIV-008-.businessFix:
1. Install a consent banner (Cookiebot, OneTrust, or Pandectes for Shopify).
2. Set all consent states to 'denied' by default.
3. Configure each consent option (analytics, ads, data sale, personalization) to only enable after explicit visitor acceptance.
4. Ask your developer to test that declining consent actually stops tracking.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-12-0-CONF-PRIV-008-.businessVerify: Your site will show a consent banner on first visit. Only visitors who explicitly accept will be fully tracked; others will have limited or no tracking.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-13-0-DATA-CONV-001.businessIssue: Your reported conversion count is inflated 887 times higher than actual purchases. Your metrics are unreliable.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-13-0-DATA-CONV-001.businessDetail: Instead of 3,126 real purchases, your tracking reports 2.7 million conversions. This false metric makes your ad spend look much more effective than it is, causes ad platforms to optimize poorly, and makes ROI calculations meaningless. You could be wasting significant ad budget based on this false data.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-13-0-DATA-CONV-001.businessFix:
1. Identify which events are being counted as conversions in Google Analytics → Admin → Key events.
2. Remove all non-purchase events (page views, clicks, form starts) from key events.
3. Keep only the 'Purchase' event as the primary conversion metric.
4. Remove or de-emphasize secondary micro-conversions like 'Add to cart' or 'Checkout start'.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-13-0-DATA-CONV-001.businessVerify: Your conversion numbers will drop dramatically and align with your actual order count. Your ad platforms will optimize correctly toward real purchases.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-15-0-DATA-ECOM-015.businessIssue: Your Google Ads conversion is firing on every page visit instead of only on purchases. Your conversion tracking is broken.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-15-0-DATA-ECOM-015.businessDetail: Every page visit is being reported as a conversion to Google Ads, even non-purchases. This makes your campaigns appear 100–1000× more effective than they actually are, causes severe overspending, and prevents ad optimization from working correctly. You're paying Google based on fake conversions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-15-0-DATA-ECOM-015.businessFix:
1. Log into Google Ads → Conversions → Website.
2. Find the conversion action that's firing too often.
3. Ask your developer to fix the trigger in Google Tag Manager so the conversion only fires on the purchase/order-confirmation page, not all pages.
4. Test by visiting a product page (should not fire) and completing a purchase (should fire).
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-15-0-DATA-ECOM-015.businessVerify: Google Ads will only report conversions on actual purchases. Your cost-per-conversion will increase, but your ad optimization will become accurate.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-16-0-DATA-PARAM-001.businessIssue: Your page tracking data is too fragmented. Every tiny variation in page URLs is recorded separately, making reports useless.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-16-0-DATA-PARAM-001.businessDetail: Instead of seeing 'Product page' visits grouped together, you see 838,000+ unique page paths because each URL contains session IDs, timestamps, or other parameters that change on every visit. This makes it impossible to see which pages drive the most traffic or revenue. Your reports show chaos instead of patterns.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-16-0-DATA-PARAM-001.businessFix:
1. Ask your developer to configure Google Tag Manager to strip query parameters (session IDs, tracking codes, etc.) from page paths.
2. Use clean page paths without UTMs or session identifiers.
3. Test by checking Google Analytics Reports → Pages to see if page paths are now grouped logically (e.g., '/products', '/checkout').
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-16-0-DATA-PARAM-001.businessVerify: Your page reports will consolidate thousands of similar pages into logical groupings. You'll see clear patterns about which pages drive traffic and revenue.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-17-0-DATA-QUAL-001.businessIssue: Critical product data fields are 99% empty. Your reports are missing the information needed to make decisions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-17-0-DATA-QUAL-001.businessDetail: Product categories, product lists, content groups, and user type data are almost entirely missing from your tracking. This makes product performance reports useless, prevents you from understanding customer behavior by segment, and makes merchandising optimization impossible. You have data volume but zero useful insights.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-17-0-DATA-QUAL-001.businessFix:
1. Verify product data in Shopify: Admin → Products and check that 'Product type', 'Collections', and 'Tags' are properly filled in.
2. Ask your developer to audit the Google Tag Manager events and confirm product data is being sent with each product event.
3. Have them check Google Analytics preview mode to confirm the product data appears in real-time tracking.
4. Fix any missing product metadata in Shopify and update the tracking configuration if data is not being passed.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-17-0-DATA-QUAL-001.businessVerify: Your Google Analytics reports will show product categories, which collections drive sales, and user segments—making merchandising and marketing decisions data-driven.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-18-0-DATA-REF-003.businessIssue: Junk and bot traffic is inflating your session counts and conversion metrics. Your data is polluted.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-18-0-DATA-REF-003.businessDetail: Your data includes thousands of fake sessions from ad networks, scrapers, and measurement systems posing as real visitors. November alone had 43,000+ bot sessions, inflating your conversion rate by 1.13 percentage points. This false traffic distorts your ROI calculations, makes ad optimization fail, and wastes reporting time on fake visitors.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-18-0-DATA-REF-003.businessFix:
1. Open Google Analytics → Admin → Data filters.
2. Create a filter to exclude bot traffic sources (specific domains and referral sources identified as fake).
3. Ask your developer to confirm which sources are confirmed bots and add them to the exclusion list.
4. Review Google Analytics historical data with the filter applied to see the true conversion and traffic numbers.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-18-0-DATA-REF-003.businessVerify: After filtering, your session count and conversion rate will drop to reflect only real human visitors. Your reports and ROI calculations will be accurate.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-19-0-DATA-REF-006.businessIssue: A single ad network measurement tool generated 43,000 fake sessions in November, inflating your traffic by 44%.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-19-0-DATA-REF-006.businessDetail: An ad network called Pangle (part of TikTok Ads) sent 43,000 fake sessions posing as real traffic. This inflated November's session count by 44%, distorted your conversion rate, and created false performance benchmarks. You've been basing business decisions on false metrics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-19-0-DATA-REF-006.businessFix:
1. Contact TikTok Business Support and ask whether these Pangle sessions were intended measurement events.
2. If confirmed as bot traffic, ask your developer to add this source to your Google Analytics referral exclusion list.
3. Create a data filter to exclude this traffic retroactively so historical reports are accurate.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-19-0-DATA-REF-006.businessVerify: November traffic will show ~43,000 fewer sessions (back to true numbers). Your conversion rate and revenue-per-visit benchmarks will be corrected.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-21-0-DATA-REP-013.businessIssue: Product category data is missing from all 792,000+ product views. You cannot segment sales by product type.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-21-0-DATA-REP-013.businessDetail: Without category data, you cannot answer: Which product lines are profitable? Which have high return rates? Which should get more inventory? You're treating all products as identical in your reports, making it impossible to optimize buying, pricing, or marketing by category.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-21-0-DATA-REP-013.businessFix:
1. Check Shopify Admin → Products and verify each product has a 'Product type' field filled in.
2. If many products are missing types, bulk-edit them to add appropriate categories.
3. Ask your developer to verify the Google Tag Manager events are sending the product type field with each product view.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-21-0-DATA-REP-013.businessVerify: Your Google Analytics reports will show breakdowns by product category, revealing which types drive the most revenue and have the best margins.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-22-0-DATA-REP-014.businessIssue: You're missing context about which page or collection each product view came from. All product activity is grouped together.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-22-0-DATA-REP-014.businessDetail: When a customer views a product, your tracking doesn't record whether it came from search results, a collection page, homepage recommendations, or ads. This prevents you from understanding which pages and lists drive the most conversions. You cannot optimize your navigation, collections, or homepage layout based on data.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-22-0-DATA-REP-014.businessFix:
1. Ask your developer to add product list tracking to your Google Tag Manager events.
2. When products are viewed on a collection page, the event should include the collection name and ID.
3. Test by visiting a collection page and confirming the collection name appears in Google Analytics real-time reports.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-22-0-DATA-REP-014.businessVerify: Your reports will show which collections, search results, and pages drive the most product views and conversions, enabling you to optimize your store layout.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-23-0-DATA-UTM-001.businessIssue: Facebook traffic is split across 11+ different source labels, making it impossible to see total Facebook ROI.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-23-0-DATA-UTM-001.businessDetail: Instead of seeing all Facebook traffic in one row, it's scattered across multiple variations, making it impossible to calculate total Facebook ROI or compare it to other channels. You're manually consolidating reports instead of using clean data. This fragmentation wastes time and hides the true value of Facebook ads.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-23-0-DATA-UTM-001.businessFix:
1. Ask your developer to add Facebook referral domains to your Google Analytics referral exclusion list.
2. Ensure all Facebook clicks pass proper UTM parameters (utm_source=facebook, utm_medium=paid).
3. Test by clicking a Facebook ad and confirming the session shows 'facebook / paid' in Google Analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-23-0-DATA-UTM-001.businessVerify: All Facebook traffic will be consolidated under a single 'facebook / paid' source, making your channel ROI reports clean and accurate.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-24-0-EXPERT-006.businessIssue: Your site is collecting personalized ad data without visitor consent. This is illegal in the EU, UK, and similar regulated regions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-24-0-EXPERT-006.businessDetail: Google's Consent Mode requires explicit consent for ad personalization and user data sharing. Your site has it hardcoded to 'yes' for everyone, violating GDPR and similar laws. This exposes you to regulatory fines (up to 4% of revenue) and customer lawsuits. Even if you fix basic consent, personalized ads won't work without proper compliance.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-24-0-EXPERT-006.businessFix:
1. Install a Google-certified consent manager (Cookiebot, OneTrust, Pandectes) that supports modern consent standards.
2. Configure it to ask for separate consent for: analytics, ads, personalized ads, and data sharing.
3. Set all to 'denied' by default and only enable after explicit visitor choice.
4. Ask your developer to integrate the manager with your Google Tag Manager and Google Ads setup.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-24-0-EXPERT-006.businessVerify: Your site will have a compliant consent banner. Visitors can choose what they want to share, and tracking will respect their choices.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-162-0-PREV-PRIOR-AG.businessIssue: No prior audit findings are available to compare. You cannot track progress on recommended fixes.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-162-0-PREV-PRIOR-AG.businessDetail: Without seeing what previous audits or consultants recommended, you cannot verify whether old issues are resolved or understand the full improvement roadmap. This creates duplicate work and missed continuity.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-162-0-PREV-PRIOR-AG.businessFix:
1. Gather any prior audit reports or consultant recommendations.
2. Share them so the current audit can compare old findings to new status.
3. Document what has been fixed and what remains outstanding.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-162-0-PREV-PRIOR-AG.businessVerify: You'll have a full history of recommendations and fixes, showing clear progress over time.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-165-0-SHOP-GA4-050.businessIssue: A single bot event from an ad network inflated November traffic by 31% and deflated your conversion rate by 1.13 percentage points.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-165-0-SHOP-GA4-050.businessDetail: 43,000 fake sessions from one ad-network measurement tool distorted November's entire performance picture. Your conversion rate looked worse, your revenue-per-visitor benchmarks are wrong, and any decisions based on November data are flawed. This fraud passed undetected until now.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-165-0-SHOP-GA4-050.businessFix:
1. Review your TikTok Ads account to confirm whether these campaigns were running in November and contact TikTok support.
2. Ask your developer to add this source to your Google Analytics referral exclusion list.
3. Re-check November reports with the filter applied to see true metrics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-165-0-SHOP-GA4-050.businessVerify: November's session count will drop 43,000, your conversion rate will improve by 1.13 percentage points, and revenue-per-session benchmarks will be corrected.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-166-0-CONF-CONV-004.businessIssue: Product views are being counted as conversions—397,000 fake conversions versus 3,100 real purchases.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-166-0-CONF-CONV-004.businessDetail: Every time a customer views a product, it's being recorded as a success metric. This makes your conversion rate appear 127× higher than it is, causes ad platforms to optimize toward product views instead of purchases, and wastes ad budget on wrong optimization.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-166-0-CONF-CONV-004.businessFix:
1. Open Google Analytics → Admin → Key events.
2. Remove 'view_item' as a key event (or demote it to secondary/non-bid conversion).
3. Keep only 'purchase' as your primary conversion metric.
4. Check Google Ads to verify the import updated.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-166-0-CONF-CONV-004.businessVerify: Your conversion count will align with real purchases. Ad platforms will optimize toward actual sales instead of page views.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-167-0-CONF-CONV-011.businessIssue: Your checkout tracking is incomplete — we can't see what happens between customers starting checkout and completing their purchase.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-167-0-CONF-CONV-011.businessDetail: When customers enter payment or shipping details during checkout, these critical steps aren't being recorded in your analytics. This means you're losing visibility into exactly where customers are abandoning the checkout process. Without this data, you can't identify whether people quit at payment, shipping, or another step. This directly impacts your ability to fix checkout problems and improve your funnel conversion rate.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-167-0-CONF-CONV-011.businessFix: 1. Check Shopify Admin → Settings → Apps and integrations to confirm Google Analytics is connected. 2. Ask your developer to implement payment and shipping step tracking in your checkout. 3. Test a purchase and verify the complete checkout funnel appears in your analytics. 4. Take a screenshot showing all checkout steps for your records.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-167-0-CONF-CONV-011.businessVerify: After the fix, you'll see payment and shipping steps appear in your checkout funnel analytics, showing exactly where customers complete or abandon their payment and delivery information.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-168-0-CONF-CUST-002.businessIssue: Your analytics aren't capturing customer type information — you can't distinguish between new and returning customers in your data.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-168-0-CONF-CUST-002.businessDetail: A critical piece of customer insight is missing: whether someone is a new customer, returning customer, or loyalty member. Without this classification, you can't analyze which customer types are most profitable or which marketing channels attract the best repeat buyers. You're making marketing decisions with incomplete information about who your customers are and whether your loyalty program is working.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-168-0-CONF-CUST-002.businessFix: 1. Work with your developer to set up customer type tracking in Google Analytics (new customer, returning customer, loyalty member). 2. Connect this data to your Shopify customer purchase history. 3. Test by placing a test purchase and checking that your customer type appears in analytics. 4. Save a screenshot of the customer type dimension.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-168-0-CONF-CUST-002.businessVerify: Once set up, you'll see customer type labels in your analytics reports, letting you compare how different customer groups behave and spend.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-169-0-CONF-CUST-003.businessIssue: Your analytics platform has no custom business metrics set up — key performance indicators you care about aren't being automatically calculated.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-169-0-CONF-CUST-003.businessDetail: You're not tracking conversion rate (purchases divided by visits) or cart abandonment rate automatically — metrics that directly show how well your store is performing. This means you have to manually calculate these numbers yourself. Standard metrics alone don't tell you whether your store is improving or declining, and you're missing easy visibility into your most important KPIs.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-169-0-CONF-CUST-003.businessFix: 1. Work with your developer to add calculated metrics for conversion rate and add-to-cart rate. 2. If you track loyalty points or custom rewards, add metrics for those as well. 3. Generate a report showing the new calculated metrics. 4. Save a screenshot for your records.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-169-0-CONF-CUST-003.businessVerify: You'll now see calculated metrics like conversion rate automatically computed in your reports, showing your business performance at a glance.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-170-0-CONF-CUST-005.businessIssue: Your analytics aren't reliably identifying whether a customer is new or returning — data is missing for 11% of customers, and you lack a proper customer classification system.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-170-0-CONF-CUST-005.businessDetail: The built-in customer classification is unreliable: over 1 in 10 customers aren't labeled correctly. You need a custom classification based on your Shopify customer history (did they buy before?). Without accurate customer identification, you can't measure which marketing channels drive your best repeat customers or understand customer lifetime value. You're flying blind on customer segmentation.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-170-0-CONF-CUST-005.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to create a customer classification based on Shopify's purchase history. 2. Send this classification to analytics on login and purchase events. 3. Test with a returning customer purchase and verify the 'returning customer' label appears. 4. Verify the fix resolves the missing-data gaps.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-170-0-CONF-CUST-005.businessVerify: Your analytics will now accurately label every customer as new or returning with no gaps, so you can reliably measure which marketing drives your best repeat buyers.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-171-0-CONF-FILT-002.businessIssue: Your analytics data isn't filtered to exclude your own testing and employee activity — your team's actions are being counted as real customer traffic.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-171-0-CONF-FILT-002.businessDetail: Every time you, your team, or QA staff test your store, those sessions count as real customer visits in your reports. Over time, this pollutes your data and makes your metrics unreliable. You can't trust your traffic numbers or conversion rates because internal testing is mixed in. This is especially problematic for smaller volumes where test traffic can be a significant percentage of total visits.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-171-0-CONF-FILT-002.businessFix: 1. Go to Google Analytics Admin → Data Settings → Data Filters. 2. Create a filter for internal traffic and enter your office IP addresses. 3. Activate the filter. 4. Take a screenshot confirming the filter is active.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-171-0-CONF-FILT-002.businessVerify: After setup, your analytics will exclude all traffic from your office and developer locations, giving you clean data that represents only real customer visits.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-172-0-CONF-FILT-003.businessIssue: Your internal traffic filter exists but is in testing mode instead of active — your office and testing traffic is still appearing in your reports.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-172-0-CONF-FILT-003.businessDetail: A filter exists to block your internal team's visits, but it's only marking traffic for identification — not actually excluding it. This means all your team's testing and internal browsing are still being counted as real customer visits. Your reports are still polluted with internal activity that skews your real business metrics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-172-0-CONF-FILT-003.businessFix: 1. Go to Google Analytics Admin → Data Settings → Data Filters. 2. Find the internal traffic filter and review its status. 3. Change its state from testing to active. 4. Save and take a screenshot.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-172-0-CONF-FILT-003.businessVerify: Your reports will now exclude internal team traffic, and your analytics numbers will reflect only real customer behavior.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-173-0-CONF-GA4-023.businessIssue: Your analytics platform has no automated alerts set up — if a critical metric like daily purchases or revenue suddenly drops, you won't be notified.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-173-0-CONF-GA4-023.businessDetail: Without alerts, a tracking problem or technical issue could silently break your data collection for days before you notice. A bot attack or unexpected traffic spike goes undetected. You have no early warning system for revenue drops or when your tracking stops working. You could be making decisions based on incorrect data without realizing it, losing money while the problem goes unnoticed.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-173-0-CONF-GA4-023.businessFix: 1. Open Google Analytics Insights. 2. Create alerts for daily purchases below 50, daily revenue below 100,000 EGP, and sessions increasing more than 200% above normal. 3. Enable email notifications for these alerts. 4. Test that you receive an alert email.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-173-0-CONF-GA4-023.businessVerify: You'll receive automatic email alerts if purchases, revenue, or traffic behave abnormally, giving you early warning of problems.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-174-0-CONF-GA4-024.businessIssue: You don't have an automated alert for sudden drops in daily purchases — if your sales tracking breaks, you might not notice for days.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-174-0-CONF-GA4-024.businessDetail: A tracking error could cause your purchase data to stop flowing into analytics without you realizing it. You might be making business decisions based on zero or artificially low sales data. This is critical for a store where you need to respond quickly to real problems. Without an alert, a critical issue could go undetected for 24–48 hours, costing you lost sales insights.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-174-0-CONF-GA4-024.businessFix: 1. Open Google Analytics Insights. 2. Create a custom alert: 'When daily purchases drop more than 40% compared to the previous week → send email'. 3. Activate the alert. 4. Take a screenshot to confirm.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-174-0-CONF-GA4-024.businessVerify: You'll receive an immediate email alert if your daily purchases suddenly drop, letting you investigate and fix tracking issues quickly.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-175-0-CONF-GA4-026.businessIssue: You don't have an alert for sudden traffic spikes — a bot attack in November that increased your traffic by 75% went completely undetected.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-175-0-CONF-GA4-026.businessDetail: Your analytics data can be corrupted by bot attacks, which inflate your traffic, artificially reduce your conversion rate, and waste your ad budget. The November bot attack shows this risk is real: 75% of your traffic was fake, but you had no way of knowing. You were making decisions based on corrupted data during that period, and false metrics skewed your understanding of store performance.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-175-0-CONF-GA4-026.businessFix: 1. Go to Google Analytics Insights. 2. Create a custom alert: 'When daily sessions increase more than 150% above the previous week → send email'. 3. Enable the alert. 4. Save and take a screenshot.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-175-0-CONF-GA4-026.businessVerify: When bot traffic or unusual spikes occur, you'll receive an immediate alert so you can investigate and prevent corrupt data from affecting your business decisions.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-176-0-CONF-INT-004.businessIssue: We can't confirm whether your purchase data is flowing from analytics to Google Ads for bidding optimization — your ad campaigns may be using incorrect conversion signals.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-176-0-CONF-INT-004.businessDetail: If Google Ads isn't receiving your purchase conversion data, your automated bidding system is working blind. Google's algorithm can't optimize your ad spend toward real purchases if it doesn't know what a purchase looks like in your data. Instead, it might be optimizing toward page views or clicks, which costs more money for lower-quality conversions. You could be wasting significant ad budget if your conversion data isn't properly connected.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-176-0-CONF-INT-004.businessFix: 1. Log into Google Ads and go to Tools → Conversions. 2. Check which conversion actions are active and confirm Google Analytics purchases are being imported. 3. Verify the conversion action is set as your primary conversion for bidding. 4. Document which conversion action Google Ads is optimizing toward.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-176-0-CONF-INT-004.businessVerify: You'll confirm that your store's purchase data is flowing to Google Ads and being used to optimize your ad spend toward real sales, not just clicks.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-177-0-CONF-INT-005.businessIssue: Your automated Google Ads campaigns may not be optimizing toward your actual purchases — they might be using low-quality conversion signals like page views instead.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-177-0-CONF-INT-005.businessDetail: Performance Max campaigns rely on conversion data to automatically optimize ad spend. If they're not getting purchase data or if they're using page views as the goal, the algorithm can't optimize correctly. This leads to overspending on low-value traffic and underspending on real purchases. Over a campaign, this misalignment can waste thousands in ad budget.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-177-0-CONF-INT-005.businessFix: 1. Check your Google Ads Performance Max campaigns. 2. View each campaign's conversion goal. 3. Verify they're using purchases as the primary conversion, not page views or other metrics. 4. Update any campaigns that aren't using purchase goals.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-177-0-CONF-INT-005.businessVerify: Your Performance Max campaigns will optimize toward actual purchases, improving your return on ad spend and reducing wasted budget on low-value traffic.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-178-0-CONF-INT-007.businessIssue: Your analytics data isn't being exported to BigQuery — you don't have a long-term backup or advanced analysis capability.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-178-0-CONF-INT-007.businessDetail: BigQuery stores your complete raw analytics data for long-term analysis and custom reports. Without it, you're limited to what Google Analytics' standard reports can show. You can't run custom analyses, can't keep data beyond the retention period, and can't build custom dashboards. This limits your ability to answer detailed business questions and creates a single point of failure if something goes wrong.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-178-0-CONF-INT-007.businessFix: 1. Go to Google Analytics Admin → Property Settings → Product Links. 2. Create a BigQuery link. 3. Enable daily export of your analytics data. 4. Verify the link is active and data is flowing.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-178-0-CONF-INT-007.businessVerify: Your store's complete analytics data will be exported daily to BigQuery, giving you a long-term archive and the ability to run custom business analyses.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-179-0-CONF-INT-008-.businessIssue: We can't confirm whether your analytics data is being exported to BigQuery, or if it is, whether it's being updated daily with fresh data.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-179-0-CONF-INT-008-.businessDetail: If BigQuery isn't linked to your analytics, no backup export is happening. Even if it is linked, data might only update weekly instead of daily, which delays your insights. Daily data export is critical for monitoring your business and responding quickly to changes. Delayed data means delayed decisions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-179-0-CONF-INT-008-.businessFix: 1. Check Google Analytics Admin → Property Settings → BigQuery Link status. 2. Confirm the link exists and is active. 3. Verify the export frequency is set to daily (or intraday for real-time dashboards). 4. Take a screenshot of the settings.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-179-0-CONF-INT-008-.businessVerify: Your analytics data will export to BigQuery every day, ensuring you always have up-to-date information for analysis and decision-making.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-180-0-CONF-INT-014.businessIssue: We can't confirm whether your Facebook pixel is using server-side conversion tracking — if Apple privacy settings block your pixel data, Facebook won't know about your purchases.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-180-0-CONF-INT-014.businessDetail: Most of your iPhone customers use iOS privacy settings that block Facebook's pixel from seeing their purchases. To reach these privacy-protected customers with retargeting ads, Facebook needs server-side data sent directly from your Shopify store. Without this backup connection, you're losing visibility on a large portion of your audience, reducing your ability to retarget and increasing your ad costs.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-180-0-CONF-INT-014.businessFix: 1. Log into Shopify Admin → Apps → Facebook & Instagram channel. 2. Enable Conversions API (server-side tracking). 3. Verify it's connected and sending events. 4. Check Facebook Events Manager to confirm server events are appearing.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-180-0-CONF-INT-014.businessVerify: Server-side conversion tracking will send your purchase data directly to Facebook, ensuring you reach iPhone users and other privacy-protected audiences with retargeting ads.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-181-0-CONF-INT-015.businessIssue: If both your Facebook pixel and server-side tracking are active, purchases might be double-counted in Facebook's reports — each purchase could be recorded twice.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-181-0-CONF-INT-015.businessDetail: When both the browser pixel and server tracking send the same purchase event, Facebook needs to recognize they're the same transaction and count it only once. Without proper deduplication using unique event IDs, you'll see inflated purchase numbers in Facebook reports, which skews your advertising performance metrics and ROI calculations. This makes it impossible to trust your Facebook analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-181-0-CONF-INT-015.businessFix: 1. Work with your developer to ensure every purchase event gets a unique ID (transaction or order number). 2. Confirm both the pixel and server tracking send this same ID for the same purchase. 3. Test a purchase and verify the ID appears in Facebook Events Manager. 4. Confirm no duplicates appear in your Facebook purchase count.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-181-0-CONF-INT-015.businessVerify: Purchases will be counted once in Facebook, not doubled, giving you accurate conversion data for ad optimization and reporting.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-182-0-CONF-PRIV-004.businessIssue: Your analytics is recording sensitive URLs like '/account' and '/checkouts' — if these URLs contain personal information or tokens, you have a privacy and security risk.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-182-0-CONF-PRIV-004.businessDetail: Checkout and account pages may contain tokens, order details, or customer information in the URL. If these are recorded in your analytics and shared with third parties, you're exposing customer data. This is a legal and privacy risk that could violate data protection laws. Additionally, these URLs don't help with business analysis — you should only track high-level page categories.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-182-0-CONF-PRIV-004.businessFix: 1. Contact your developer and ask them to check checkout URLs for any tokens or customer data. 2. Sanitize URLs before they're sent to analytics by removing tokens and sensitive parameters. 3. Replace detailed checkout URLs with generic categories. 4. Verify the fix by checking a test purchase and confirming no sensitive data appears in the analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-182-0-CONF-PRIV-004.businessVerify: Your analytics will no longer record sensitive account or checkout URLs, protecting customer privacy and reducing your legal and security risk.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-184-0-DATA-CONV-004.businessIssue: Your paid search ads show almost no revenue in analytics — the link between Google Ads and your analytics is broken or not working properly.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-184-0-DATA-CONV-004.businessDetail: You've been running Google Ads campaigns, but your analytics isn't crediting those ads with any revenue. This means you can't measure which ads are actually profitable, so you can't optimize your spending. You might be spending thousands on ads while analytics shows they're doing nothing. Without proper attribution, you can't make smart budget decisions or know where to allocate your ad money.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-184-0-DATA-CONV-004.businessFix: 1. Go to Google Ads Account Settings → Auto-tagging → enable auto-tagging. 2. Go to Google Analytics Admin → Google Ads Links → confirm the link is active and auto-tagging is enabled. 3. Confirm your Google Ads campaigns were active during Feb–May 2025. 4. If auto-tagging was just enabled, wait 24–48 hours for data to appear. 5. Re-run your analytics reports to see if ad revenue now appears.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-184-0-DATA-CONV-004.businessVerify: Your paid search ads will now show proper revenue and conversion credit in analytics, so you can measure ad performance accurately and optimize your budget.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-185-0-DATA-CONV-009.businessIssue: Your 'key events' metric is meaningless for business decisions — it's counting page views and scrolls instead of just purchases.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-185-0-DATA-CONV-009.businessDetail: Your analytics reports over 1 million 'key events' but only 856 actual purchases — a completely disconnected ratio that makes the metric worthless. You can't use this metric to compare which marketing channels drive the most valuable traffic or make budget decisions. The numbers are so inflated they're useless for any business purpose.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-185-0-DATA-CONV-009.businessFix: 1. Stop using 'key events' as your main metric for decision-making. 2. Use purchases and purchase revenue as your primary metrics instead. 3. Go to Google Analytics Admin → Events → ensure only 'purchase' is marked as a key event. 4. Update your Google Ads conversion import to use only 'purchase'.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-185-0-DATA-CONV-009.businessVerify: Your reports will now show meaningful metrics (purchases and revenue) that actually represent your business performance, not inflated interaction counts.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-186-0-DATA-CONV-010.businessIssue: Your checkout funnel is broken — customers go from starting checkout to completing purchase with no visibility into payment or shipping steps in between.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-186-0-DATA-CONV-010.businessDetail: You see 64,000 add-to-cart actions but only 3,000 purchases — an 80% drop with no insight into where it's happening. You have no idea which step customers drop off at (payment, shipping, confirmation). Without this breakdown, you can't identify which checkout problem is costing you the most sales. This is critical data for improving your funnel and recovering lost revenue.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-186-0-DATA-CONV-010.businessFix: 1. Work with your developer to add payment and shipping step events to your Shopify checkout. 2. Implement tracking for payment and shipping info events. 3. Test a purchase and verify both steps appear in your analytics. 4. Once verified, re-run your funnel report to see the complete picture.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-186-0-DATA-CONV-010.businessVerify: Your checkout funnel will show all steps, letting you see exactly where customers drop off and what needs fixing to recover lost sales.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-187-0-DATA-ECOM-002.businessIssue: Your analytics is showing duplicate transaction IDs — some purchases are being counted two or more times, inflating your revenue numbers.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-187-0-DATA-ECOM-002.businessDetail: If a purchase is recorded twice in analytics, it inflates your revenue, distorts your conversion metrics, and gives you false confidence in your store's performance. You might be actually making less money than your reports show. You can't trust your revenue numbers or conversion rates until you find and fix what's causing duplicates.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-187-0-DATA-ECOM-002.businessFix: 1. Use a data analysis tool to find orders with duplicate transaction IDs. 2. Cross-reference those IDs against Shopify's order records to see if they're real duplicates or data errors. 3. Work with your developer to fix the source of the duplicates. 4. Verify duplicates are resolved.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-187-0-DATA-ECOM-002.businessVerify: Your transaction data will be cleaned so each purchase is counted only once, giving you accurate revenue and conversion numbers you can trust.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-188-0-DATA-ECOM-004.businessIssue: Your tracking code is sending product data with inconsistent field names, so Google Analytics can't read the product information correctly.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-188-0-DATA-ECOM-004.businessDetail: When product data is incomplete or uses the wrong field names, Google Analytics can't generate accurate reports on which products are selling, which categories are popular, or which promotions are working. This means you can't see real performance data, and your ad platform can't optimize bidding for your best sellers. You may be wasting budget on ads for products that aren't actually driving sales. Without clean product data, you'll make business decisions based on incomplete or misleading reports.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-188-0-DATA-ECOM-004.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to check how product information is being sent to Google Analytics (check the source code or Google Tag Manager configuration). 2. Ensure all products send these fields: product ID, product name, product category, product variant, price, and discount. 3. Test by purchasing a product and verifying the data appears correctly in Google Analytics reports.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-188-0-DATA-ECOM-004.businessVerify: After the fix, your Google Analytics reports will show accurate product names, categories, and prices, and you'll be able to see which products and categories drive the most revenue.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-189-0-DATA-ECOM-005.businessIssue: Your tracking is not recording which page section (collection, search results, homepage feature) customers browse products from, so you can't see which sections drive sales.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-189-0-DATA-ECOM-005.businessDetail: Without knowing where customers discovered each product, you can't tell if your featured collections are effective, if your search feature helps customers buy, or which homepage sections drive the most revenue. This means you can't optimize the placement and promotion of your best sellers, and you may be spending time and budget on page sections that don't actually drive sales. You're essentially flying blind on page performance.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-189-0-DATA-ECOM-005.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to add 'product list name' to all product tracking (e.g., 'Homepage Featured', 'Search Results', 'Collection: Natural Oils'). 2. Update the tracking in your store's code or Google Tag Manager to label each product with its source section. 3. Verify by visiting different sections (featured products, search results, collections) and checking that Google Analytics records the section name.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-189-0-DATA-ECOM-005.businessVerify: After the fix, you'll see in Google Analytics which page sections drive the most product views and purchases, so you can optimize your homepage and collections.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-190-0-DATA-ECOM-006.businessIssue: Your tracking is not recording the quantity of each product customers buy, so you're undercounting some sales and missing bulk-purchase insights.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-190-0-DATA-ECOM-006.businessDetail: If a customer orders 5 bottles of one product and 2 bottles of another, your current tracking counts that as 2 products sold instead of 7. This distorts your sales reports, makes your revenue numbers look lower than they actually are, and prevents you from seeing which products sell in bulk. You can't identify your best bulk-sellers or offer bundle discounts effectively. Your ad platform also can't optimize for high-value bulk purchases.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-190-0-DATA-ECOM-006.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to include the order quantity for each product in your tracking data. 2. Update the tracking in your shopping cart and checkout to send how many items of each product are in the order. 3. Test by placing an order with multiple quantities of one product and verifying the quantity appears in Google Analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-190-0-DATA-ECOM-006.businessVerify: After the fix, Google Analytics will show the actual number of items sold, not just the number of different products, and you'll see accurate revenue and bulk-purchase patterns.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-191-0-DATA-ECOM-011.businessIssue: In November 2025, automatic bot traffic inflated your page views and made your actual sales rate look much worse than it really is.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-191-0-DATA-ECOM-011.businessDetail: Bots viewing your products without buying skew your reports. A 4% conversion rate might actually be 5% or higher if you remove the bot traffic. This false data affects your decisions: you may cut ad spend when performance is actually good, or invest in optimization when you should be scaling. You're also wasting money on ads that look ineffective because bots are inflating the page-view count without ever buying. Until you clean the data, you can't trust your November metrics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-191-0-DATA-ECOM-011.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer or Google Analytics support to add a filter to exclude the bot traffic source. 2. Apply the filter to a new report view so you can see the real November data without bots. 3. Compare the before and after numbers to confirm your true conversion rate and ROI.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-191-0-DATA-ECOM-011.businessVerify: After the fix, your November reports will show the real conversion rate and revenue without bot inflation, so you can trust your metrics and make better decisions.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-192-0-DATA-ECOM-013.businessIssue: Google free shopping results from your store are being labeled as paid search traffic, so you can't see how much revenue comes from free vs. paid search.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-192-0-DATA-ECOM-013.businessDetail: Free listings on Google Shopping are a source of free traffic and sales. If they're mislabeled as paid ads, your ad performance metrics are inflated, and you may overestimate your paid search return. You might spend more on ads thinking they're working better than they are, or cut ad budget based on inflated performance numbers. You also can't see the true value of your free Google listings, so you can't decide whether to invest in promoting them. Your ad budget optimization will be based on wrong data.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-192-0-DATA-ECOM-013.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to set up a rule in Google Analytics to correctly identify free Google shopping traffic (reclassify organic shopping revenue as organic or create a dedicated 'Google Free Shopping' label). 2. Update this rule in Google Analytics settings (Admin → Channel grouping). 3. Re-run your reports to see the corrected breakdown of free vs. paid search.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-192-0-DATA-ECOM-013.businessVerify: After the fix, your Google Analytics will show free shopping traffic separately from paid ads, so you can see the true value of each channel and optimize ad spend correctly.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-193-0-DATA-ECOM-014.businessIssue: Your tracking is missing the traffic source for 226 sales, so you don't know where those customers came from or how to repeat that success.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-193-0-DATA-ECOM-014.businessDetail: When you don't know the source of your sales, you can't optimize your marketing. You might be getting customers from a channel you haven't even measured, or losing budget to a channel that isn't working. These 226 missing transactions could be from your best-performing ad campaign or your worst-performing channel—you won't know until you fix it. Without this data, you're making budget decisions blind.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-193-0-DATA-ECOM-014.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to investigate why 226 transactions have no traffic source recorded (check if certain payment methods, devices, or browsers are affected). 2. Fix the tracking to ensure all orders record their traffic source. 3. Re-import or refresh the data in Google Analytics to capture the missing source information.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-193-0-DATA-ECOM-014.businessVerify: After the fix, all sales will have a recorded source, so you can see which channels drive each transaction and optimize your budget accordingly.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-194-0-DATA-ECOM-016.businessIssue: Your Google Ads is counting product page views as sales, which makes your ad performance look much better than it really is and breaks your ad bidding strategy.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-194-0-DATA-ECOM-016.businessDetail: When Google Ads counts views instead of purchases, your conversion numbers are 10–50× higher than real sales. This inflates your return on ad spend numbers, so you may spend more on ads than you should or bid too high. Your automated bidding breaks because it's optimizing for fake conversions, not real purchases. You end up overpaying for clicks that don't lead to sales. Your true ad return is much lower than your reports show.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-194-0-DATA-ECOM-016.businessFix: 1. Open Google Ads and check the conversion tracking for product views. Change it to track only purchase events, not product views. 2. Or remove this tracking label entirely and use only your main purchase conversion. 3. Wait 24 hours for data to update, then check that your conversion counts match your actual order count in Shopify.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-194-0-DATA-ECOM-016.businessVerify: After the fix, your Google Ads conversion count will match your actual purchase count (from your Shopify order history), so your ad return will be accurate.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-195-0-DATA-ECOM-019.businessIssue: Your tracking is missing key product information (brand, category, variant), so Google Analytics can't show you which specific products, variants, or categories are driving sales.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-195-0-DATA-ECOM-019.businessDetail: Without product-level data, you can't see which variant (size, color, scent) sells best, which brand performs well, or which category generates the most revenue. You're forced to make decisions based on total sales instead of item-by-item performance. You can't identify slow-moving inventory, optimize product pricing by type, or forecast demand by variant. You're missing the data you need to improve product performance and reduce wasted inventory.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-195-0-DATA-ECOM-019.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to add these fields to all product tracking: product brand, product category, product variant (size, color, scent), and product SKU. 2. Update the tracking configuration in Google Tag Manager or your store's code to include these fields from Shopify. 3. Test by placing a purchase and verifying all product fields appear in Google Analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-195-0-DATA-ECOM-019.businessVerify: After the fix, Google Analytics will show detailed reports on product brand, category, and variant performance, so you can optimize your product mix.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-196-0-DATA-EVT-002.businessIssue: Your tracking is not recording when customers view product pages, so you can't see which products customers are interested in or how your catalog performs.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-196-0-DATA-EVT-002.businessDetail: Product views are the first step in your sales funnel. If you're not tracking them, you can't see which products attract the most interest, which product pages have high bounce rates, or which products convert visitors into buyers. You miss the opportunity to optimize product descriptions, images, or pricing based on engagement. You also can't calculate your conversion rate (buyers ÷ viewers) for each product, which is essential for identifying problem products.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-196-0-DATA-EVT-002.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to add product-view tracking to your product pages (implement the 'view item' event in Google Tag Manager or your store's code). 2. Test by visiting several product pages and confirming the views appear in Google Analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-196-0-DATA-EVT-002.businessVerify: After the fix, Google Analytics will show how many customers viewed each product and how many bought, so you can optimize products with low conversion rates.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-197-0-DATA-EVT-010.businessIssue: Your tracking is not recording when customers add items to their cart, so you can't see how many people abandon shopping before checkout.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-197-0-DATA-EVT-010.businessDetail: Without cart tracking, you can't measure cart abandonment—a critical metric for e-commerce. If 100 customers add items to their cart but only 50 complete purchase, you're missing that 50% are abandoning. This metric tells you if your checkout process is too long, payment options are limited, or shipping costs surprise customers. You can't fix the problem if you can't measure it. You're potentially leaving thousands in lost revenue on the table without even knowing.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-197-0-DATA-EVT-010.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to add cart tracking to your store (implement 'view cart' event in Google Tag Manager or your store's code). 2. Test by adding items to your cart and verifying the event appears in Google Analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-197-0-DATA-EVT-010.businessVerify: After the fix, Google Analytics will show your cart abandonment rate, so you can identify and fix checkout problems.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-198-0-DATA-EVT-011.businessIssue: Your tracking is not recording refunds, so your revenue reports don't show the net amount customers actually kept.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-198-0-DATA-EVT-011.businessDetail: If you don't track refunds, your revenue looks higher than it actually is. Refunds are a sign of customer dissatisfaction, product problems, or returns. Without this data, you can't see which products have high return rates, which might indicate quality issues. You can't identify patterns like refunds spiking after certain marketing campaigns, which might reveal customer expectation mismatches. Your financials and product decisions are based on incomplete information.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-198-0-DATA-EVT-011.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to add refund tracking from your Shopify account to Google Analytics (implement 'refund' event). 2. Test by processing a refund and verifying it appears in Google Analytics within 24 hours.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-198-0-DATA-EVT-011.businessVerify: After the fix, Google Analytics will show your net revenue (after refunds) and which products have high return rates, so you can improve customer satisfaction.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-199-0-DATA-PARAM-00.businessIssue: Your page URLs contain too many unique IDs (likely product or session numbers), which breaks your page-level reports and makes it impossible to see which pages drive traffic.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-199-0-DATA-PARAM-00.businessDetail: If every page view gets a unique URL due to product IDs or session tokens, Google Analytics treats each URL as a different page. Instead of seeing 'product page' traffic, you see 43,000 unique pages, none with enough data to analyze. This breaks all your page performance reports, makes your analytics dashboard useless, and prevents you from optimizing landing pages or content. You can't see which pages convert best because the data is too fragmented.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-199-0-DATA-PARAM-00.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to investigate what creates unique URLs (product IDs, session tokens, query parameters) and remove or standardize them. 2. Configure Google Analytics to group similar pages (e.g., all /product/* URLs should report as one page type, not 43,000 individual URLs). 3. Verify by checking Google Analytics to see if page counts drop from 43,000+ to 200–500 realistic pages.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-199-0-DATA-PARAM-00.businessVerify: After the fix, Google Analytics will show realistic page counts, so you can analyze which pages and page types drive the most traffic and revenue.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-201-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessIssue: Your products are not tagged with categories in Google Analytics, so you can't see which product categories are driving sales.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-201-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessDetail: Without category data, you can't answer basic questions: Which of your product categories (e.g., Natural Oils, Skincare, Gifts) generates the most revenue? Which category has the highest profit margin? Are certain categories seasonal? You're forced to analyze sales product-by-product instead of by business line. This makes inventory planning, pricing, and promotional decisions much harder. You can't optimize your marketing budget by category.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-201-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to send product categories from Shopify to Google Analytics with every purchase or product view. 2. Map your Shopify product types or collections to category names (e.g., 'Natural Oils', 'Skincare', 'Gifts'). 3. Verify by placing a purchase and checking that the product category appears in Google Analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-201-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessVerify: After the fix, Google Analytics will show revenue, conversions, and traffic broken down by product category, so you can optimize by business line.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-202-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessIssue: Products are not labeled with which section of your store they came from (featured products, collections, search results), so you can't optimize your store layout.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-202-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessDetail: Without knowing which section customers browse from, you can't measure the effectiveness of your homepage featured products, collections, or search feature. You might have high-value featured sections that drive few sales, or collections no one visits. You can't optimize your store navigation or decide where to place new products. You're also missing a key signal for improving product recommendations and layout testing.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-202-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to tag all products with their source section (e.g., 'Homepage Featured', 'Collection: Natural Oils', 'Search Results'). 2. Update your store's tracking or Google Tag Manager to include section names with product data. 3. Verify by visiting different sections and checking that Google Analytics records the section name.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-202-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessVerify: After the fix, Google Analytics will show which sections drive the most traffic and revenue, so you can optimize your homepage, collections, and search results.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-203-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessIssue: Google Analytics loads twice on your homepage, which doubles the page view count and makes your homepage metrics unreliable.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-203-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessDetail: When a tracking code loads twice, you're counting each page view twice, so your homepage looks twice as popular as it actually is. This inflates conversion rates, makes your ROI calculations wrong, and breaks your ad platforms' view tracking. If you're using automated bidding, it optimizes based on fake metrics. This also creates inconsistent data between Google Analytics and your ads platform, which makes debugging impossible.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-203-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to check your website code and Google Tag Manager to find why the tracking code loads twice on the homepage. 2. Remove the duplicate code or Google Tag Manager rule so it only loads once per page. 3. Clear your browser cache and verify on the homepage that the tracking code loads only once.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-203-0-DATA-PARAM-01.businessVerify: After the fix, homepage page views will be half of what they were before (because you're no longer double-counting), and your metrics will match your other pages.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-204-0-DATA-PII-008.businessIssue: Your store is automatically sending hashed email addresses to Meta (Facebook) on every page view, even when no customer is logged in, which creates privacy and legal risk.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-204-0-DATA-PII-008.businessDetail: Sending customer email data to ad platforms without clear consent violates privacy laws in many countries, including European regulations. Even hashed emails count as personal data. If your customers are not logged in, the email source is unclear—you might be sending the owner's email or test data to Meta. This exposes your business to compliance fines and customer trust issues. You need to only send email when a customer is logged in and has consented to tracking.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-204-0-DATA-PII-008.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to disable advanced email matching on the Meta Pixel (or configure it to only fire when a customer is logged in). 2. Ensure the pixel only sends email data after the customer logs in or opts in to tracking. 3. Test by visiting the site on a clean browser (not logged in) and verifying no email is sent to Meta.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-204-0-DATA-PII-008.businessVerify: After the fix, email data will only be sent to Meta when customers are logged in and have consented, protecting your business from compliance risk.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-205-0-DATA-QUAL-001.businessIssue: Google Analytics is double-counting homepage page views, which makes your homepage appear twice as popular as it really is and breaks your data reliability.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-205-0-DATA-QUAL-001.businessDetail: When a tracking code fires twice on a page, metrics become unreliable. Your homepage conversion rate will look artificially high, and you can't trust any decisions based on homepage data. This inconsistency creates friction with ad platforms and makes debugging analytics issues nearly impossible. Reports between Google Analytics and Google Ads won't match, and you lose confidence in your metrics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-205-0-DATA-QUAL-001.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer to check the website code and Google Tag Manager for duplicate tracking code on the homepage. 2. Remove the duplicate so the tracking code loads only once per page. 3. Verify by comparing homepage page-view counts to inner pages. Homepage should not have 2× the page views.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-205-0-DATA-QUAL-001.businessVerify: After the fix, homepage page views will be accurate and match other pages, and your metrics will be reliable again.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-206-0-DATA-QUAL-002.businessIssue: In November, automatic bot traffic from one source inflated your page views and made your real conversion rate look much worse than it actually was.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-206-0-DATA-QUAL-002.businessDetail: Bots viewing products without buying skew your reports. If 98,000 bots visited in November but only 2,000 real customers did, your conversion rate looks 50× worse than reality. This false data could cause you to panic, cut ad budgets, or make wrong business decisions. You might stop a campaign or discount your products thinking they're not working, when the real problem is bot pollution. Until you clean this data, you can't trust any decision based on November 2025 metrics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-206-0-DATA-QUAL-002.businessFix: 1. Ask your developer or Google Analytics support to identify and filter out the bot traffic source. 2. Create a filter or view in Google Analytics to exclude this bot source from all future reports. 3. Re-analyze your November data without bot traffic to see your true conversion rate and ROI.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-206-0-DATA-QUAL-002.businessVerify: After the fix, your November reports will show the real conversion rate and ROI without bot inflation, so you can trust your historical data.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-207-0-DATA-QUAL-004.businessIssue: You have no notes in Google Analytics documenting important business events like bot attacks, campaigns, or product launches, so you can't correlate them with your data.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-207-0-DATA-QUAL-004.businessDetail: Without annotations, when you see a traffic spike or drop, you don't know why. Did a marketing campaign cause it? Was there a bot attack? Did you change pricing? These notes help you understand what caused metric changes and avoid making false conclusions. Missing this context makes analytics less useful and leads to wrong business decisions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-207-0-DATA-QUAL-004.businessFix: 1. Open Google Analytics and go to Admin → Annotations. 2. Add notes for major events: bot attack detected (November 2025), all marketing campaigns, pricing changes, product launches, and site problems. 3. Add at least 1–2 annotations per month going forward so you document your business.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-207-0-DATA-QUAL-004.businessVerify: After adding annotations, when you see traffic changes in Google Analytics, you'll immediately understand what caused them and make better decisions.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-208-0-DATA-QUAL-005.businessIssue: You have no automated alerts in Google Analytics, so you won't know if your sales or traffic suddenly drops until you manually check the reports.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-208-0-DATA-QUAL-005.businessDetail: Without alerts, you're monitoring analytics passively. A major problem (bot attack, tracking code failure, campaign performance drop) could go unnoticed for days. By the time you manually check Google Analytics, you've lost revenue and wasted ad budget. Automated alerts notify you immediately when something goes wrong, so you can fix it before it costs you money. You should have alerts for daily sales drops, revenue drops, paid traffic changes, and unusual traffic patterns.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-208-0-DATA-QUAL-005.businessFix: 1. Open Google Analytics and go to Admin → Alerts. 2. Create alerts for: (1) Daily purchases drop >30% week-over-week, (2) Daily revenue drop >30% week-over-week, (3) Paid traffic sessions drop >40% week-over-week, (4) Bounce rate spike >20% week-over-week. 3. Verify the alerts are active and will send you notifications.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-208-0-DATA-QUAL-005.businessVerify: After creating alerts, you'll receive notifications when your metrics change significantly, so you can respond quickly to problems.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-209-0-DATA-QUAL-006.businessIssue: Your November analytics show 141,770 sessions (75.7% higher than October), but this spike came from automated bot traffic that wasn't detected or documented.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-209-0-DATA-QUAL-006.businessDetail: Bot traffic artificially inflates your session counts, making your website look more popular than it actually is. This skews your reports on what products customers are viewing, which channels drive real sales, and how well your marketing campaigns perform. You may have made budget decisions based on false activity. Without identifying and removing this traffic, your reports remain unreliable.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-209-0-DATA-QUAL-006.businessFix:
1. Review your analytics for November 2025 to identify the bot traffic source and timing.
2. Apply a filter in your analytics to exclude this bot traffic from future reports.
3. Document what happened with a note in your analytics so your team knows the data was compromised.
4. Create a backup report excluding November to see what real traffic looked like.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-209-0-DATA-QUAL-006.businessVerify: After the fix, your November session count will drop to match real visitor activity, and your analytics will show consistent month-over-month growth without the artificial spike.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-210-0-DATA-QUAL-008.businessIssue: Your analytics shows 223,644 sessions (35.4%) from Android app browsers that may not be tracking correctly because they don't use standard tracking rules like regular browsers.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-210-0-DATA-QUAL-008.businessDetail: App browsers don't handle tracking cookies the same way as regular browsers, so you're losing data on how these customers navigate your store, what products they view, and whether they complete purchases. This breaks the connection between your app traffic and actual sales, making it impossible to measure if your app is working. If you're running app-based marketing campaigns, you can't see the real impact.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-210-0-DATA-QUAL-008.businessFix:
1. Test a purchase on an Android phone through the app's built-in browser and verify the sale shows up in your analytics.
2. Check your analytics settings to ensure app browser traffic is configured to send proper tracking data.
3. If tracking is missing, work with your developer to add mobile app tracking code to your store.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-210-0-DATA-QUAL-008.businessVerify: After the fix, purchases made in the app browser will show up in your sales reports with proper customer and product details, not as incomplete or missing data.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-211-0-DATA-REF-002.businessIssue: Your analytics shows 3,658 visitors with no recorded entry page, representing the 13th largest traffic source by volume — you're missing data for one of your biggest customer groups.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-211-0-DATA-REF-002.businessDetail: When the entry page is missing, you can't see where customers started their journey, which traffic sources are actually working, or which pages need improvement. With a 96.9% bounce rate and only 8 sales from 3,658 visits, you're losing visibility on a customer segment that may be high-intent. Your marketing reports are blind to this traffic source, and you can't optimize campaigns that send to unknown landing pages.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-211-0-DATA-REF-002.businessFix:
1. Check your analytics settings to find where landing page data should be recorded.
2. Verify your Shopify store and tracking code are properly configured to capture page URLs.
3. Review your web server logs or Shopify traffic sources to identify where these 3,658 sessions came from.
4. Update your tracking configuration to capture landing page data for all sessions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-211-0-DATA-REF-002.businessVerify: After the fix, every session will show its entry page, and you'll be able to see which landing pages drive sales and which ones need improvement.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-212-0-DATA-REF-005.businessIssue: Your WhatsApp traffic (1,179 visitors, 96 sales) is showing up as generic referral traffic instead of being clearly labeled as WhatsApp — hiding this high-performing channel.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-212-0-DATA-REF-005.businessDetail: WhatsApp is likely one of your best-converting channels (8.1% conversion rate), but because it's mislabeled as 'referral,' you can't see it separately in your analytics. This means you're not tracking which WhatsApp campaigns work best, you can't measure ROI on WhatsApp efforts, and you might reduce budget on a channel that's actually outperforming other social media. You're leaving money on the table by not being able to optimize WhatsApp separately.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-212-0-DATA-REF-005.businessFix:
1. List all WhatsApp links you share with customers (broadcast messages, link-in-bio, integration links).
2. Add tracking codes to these links so they're labeled as coming from WhatsApp.
3. Test a WhatsApp link to verify it arrives with correct tracking and shows as WhatsApp traffic in your reports.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-212-0-DATA-REF-005.businessVerify: After the fix, WhatsApp traffic will appear as its own channel in your reports, and you'll be able to see which WhatsApp campaigns drive the most sales.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-213-0-DATA-REF-010.businessIssue: Your analytics shows 14,004 sessions from Facebook's tracking domain classified as generic referral traffic — these are likely paid ads that should be attributed to Facebook.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-213-0-DATA-REF-010.businessDetail: By not properly tagging your paid Facebook ads, you're losing EGP 557,904 in revenue that should be attributed to your Facebook advertising channel. Your ad manager can't measure which Facebook campaigns actually drive sales, so you can't optimize ad spend. You might be scaling budgets based on incomplete data or cutting ads that are actually working. Facebook's own reporting may show different numbers, making it impossible to reconcile actual ROI.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-213-0-DATA-REF-010.businessFix:
1. Add Facebook's tracking domain to your analytics exclusion list so it's not counted as a separate referral source.
2. Ensure all Facebook ad campaigns include proper tracking codes labeled as Facebook paid traffic.
3. Re-run your Facebook ads with proper tracking and verify clicks show up correctly in your reports.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-213-0-DATA-REF-010.businessVerify: After the fix, paid Facebook traffic will be grouped under 'Facebook' with revenue clearly attributed, making it easy to see your Facebook advertising ROI.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-214-0-DATA-REF-011.businessIssue: Your analytics shows 22,454 mobile Facebook sessions classified as referral traffic instead of properly labeled Facebook traffic — losing visibility into another large revenue source.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-214-0-DATA-REF-011.businessDetail: This mislabeling represents EGP 606,086 in revenue not being correctly attributed to Facebook in your channel reports. You can't see which Facebook campaigns or audience segments drive mobile sales, so you're making ad decisions blind. Your team may be overestimating organic referral traffic and underestimating Facebook's actual contribution to revenue, leading to poor budget allocation.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-214-0-DATA-REF-011.businessFix:
1. Add mobile Facebook's domain to your analytics exclusion list.
2. Verify all Facebook mobile campaigns are tagged with proper tracking codes for Facebook.
3. Test a Facebook ad link on mobile and confirm it shows as Facebook traffic in your reports.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-214-0-DATA-REF-011.businessVerify: After the fix, mobile Facebook traffic will merge with your other Facebook traffic under one 'Facebook' channel, showing the true revenue impact of your mobile ad campaigns.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-215-0-DATA-REF-012.businessIssue: Your analytics shows 7,821 customers from Facebook Messenger being counted as generic referral traffic — separating them from your Facebook channel tracking.
