Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-215-0-DATA-REF-012.businessDetail: Messenger is a direct sales channel (315 transactions, EGP 225,048), but by not properly tracking it, you can't measure which Messenger campaigns drive sales or whether the channel is worth investment. Your team can't see Messenger's contribution to revenue separately, making it impossible to optimize messaging strategy. You have no way to track which messages convert to sales.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-215-0-DATA-REF-012.businessFix:
1. Identify all Messenger links and broadcast messages you send to customers.
2. Update those links with tracking codes labeled as Facebook Messenger traffic.
3. Add the Messenger domain to your analytics exclusion list.
4. Verify a Messenger link shows correctly in your analytics after updating.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-215-0-DATA-REF-012.businessVerify: After the fix, Messenger traffic will appear as its own tracked source, and you'll be able to measure which messages and campaigns drive the most sales.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-216-0-DATA-REF-013.businessIssue: Your analytics shows 8,394 Instagram visitors being labeled as referral traffic instead of Instagram traffic — hiding another key social media revenue source.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-216-0-DATA-REF-013.businessDetail: Instagram is driving 445 sales (EGP 325,798) but your reports can't show which Instagram campaigns work, whether your ads outperform organic posts, or what return you're getting. You can't measure if Instagram is worth the content investment or ad spend, and your social media team can't optimize the Instagram strategy based on sales data.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-216-0-DATA-REF-013.businessFix:
1. Add Instagram's tracking domain to your analytics exclusion list.
2. Ensure all Instagram ad campaigns have proper tracking codes labeled as Instagram paid traffic.
3. Create trackable links for organic Instagram posts labeled as Instagram social traffic.
4. Test an Instagram link to confirm it arrives with proper tracking.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-216-0-DATA-REF-013.businessVerify: After the fix, Instagram traffic will show as its own channel in your reports, with sales clearly attributed so you can measure both paid and organic performance.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-217-0-DATA-REP-001.businessIssue: Your analytics report is collapsing certain data into an '(other)' category because you're tracking too many variations of the same thing — hiding important details.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-217-0-DATA-REP-001.businessDetail: When similar items get grouped into '(other),' your reports become useless. You can't see which specific products, pages, or campaigns are performing; instead you see 'other products' which tells you nothing. This makes it harder to spot trends, identify bestsellers, or find underperforming areas. Your marketing and product teams lose visibility into what actually matters.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-217-0-DATA-REP-001.businessFix:
1. Review your analytics to find which reports show large '(other)' categories.
2. Identify what data is being grouped together (pages, products, search terms).
3. Reduce the number of unique tracking variations (consistent product names, avoid multiple URL formats for the same page).
4. Update your tracking to consolidate similar items into clean categories.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-217-0-DATA-REP-001.businessVerify: After the fix, your reports will show specific details instead of '(other)' categories, revealing which products and pages are actually driving sales.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-218-0-DATA-REP-002.businessIssue: Your landing page report is showing the same page multiple times with different tracking codes attached, fragmenting your data and making it hard to measure real page performance.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-218-0-DATA-REP-002.businessDetail: When the same page appears as multiple entries (with tracking code, without, with different parameters), you can't see total traffic to that page. Your team can't identify which pages drive the most traffic or conversions, making optimization impossible. You're spending money driving traffic to pages that appear underperforming in reports, when they might actually be performing well if the data wasn't split.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-218-0-DATA-REP-002.businessFix:
1. Audit your most-trafficked landing pages in your analytics report.
2. Check if the same page appears multiple times with different URLs or parameters.
3. Configure your tracking to strip temporary tracking codes from page URLs.
4. Consolidate duplicate page entries so each page shows once with combined traffic.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-218-0-DATA-REP-002.businessVerify: After the fix, each landing page will appear once in your reports with all traffic combined, showing the true performance of each page.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-219-0-DATA-REP-004.businessIssue: Your detailed analytics reports are too large to process fully, so the system is showing you estimates instead of exact numbers — reducing report accuracy.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-219-0-DATA-REP-004.businessDetail: When your analytics data is too large or fragmented, the system automatically samples it, meaning you're viewing estimates rather than real numbers. Your reports might be off by 5-20%, and you won't know it. This leads to wrong decisions: you might think a campaign is working when the sample doesn't represent reality, or scale budget based on incomplete data. The bigger your traffic grows, the worse the sampling becomes.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-219-0-DATA-REP-004.businessFix:
1. Reduce the number of unique pages, products, and tracking variations in your store (consolidate as much as possible).
2. Upgrade to connect your analytics to Google's data warehouse to enable unsampled reports.
3. Focus detailed analysis on smaller date ranges rather than long periods to avoid triggering sampling.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-219-0-DATA-REP-004.businessVerify: After the fix, your reports will show exact numbers without sampling, so you can make decisions with confidence that the data is accurate.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-220-0-DATA-REP-005.businessIssue: Your analytics is not set up to track custom business data — you're only seeing basic visitor metrics without insights into your products or customers.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-220-0-DATA-REP-005.businessDetail: You can't segment reports by product category, brand, or variant without custom tracking. This means you can't answer questions like 'which product categories drive the most revenue' or 'how do different brands perform.' Your team is limited to generic reports that don't match your business structure, making it hard to optimize product strategy or identify bestsellers.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-220-0-DATA-REP-005.businessFix:
1. Define what custom product data matters most to your business (category, brand, variant, product line).
2. Work with your developer to add this data to your tracking implementation.
3. Register these as custom dimensions in your analytics settings.
4. Test by purchasing a few products and verifying custom data appears in your reports.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-220-0-DATA-REP-005.businessVerify: After the fix, your analytics will track and display your custom product categories and attributes, allowing you to see which product types drive the most sales.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-221-0-DATA-REP-007.businessIssue: Your analytics data is not being exported to a data warehouse, so you can only use basic reports and can't do advanced analysis.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-221-0-DATA-REP-007.businessDetail: Without data warehouse access, you can't run unsampled reports on large datasets, you can't combine analytics data with other business systems, and you can't build custom reports that answer your specific business questions. You're locked into standard reports. If you want deep insights or want to combine web analytics with your sales database or inventory system, you can't.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-221-0-DATA-REP-007.businessFix:
1. Upgrade to connect your analytics to Google's data warehouse (requires a cloud account).
2. Enable daily or streaming export of your analytics data.
3. Test the connection and verify data is flowing to your data warehouse.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-221-0-DATA-REP-007.businessVerify: After the fix, your complete analytics data will be available for advanced analysis, enabling custom reports and full-data accuracy on large date ranges.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-222-0-DATA-REP-008.businessIssue: Your search report doesn't show what customers are actually searching for on your site, limiting your ability to understand customer intent and improve navigation.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-222-0-DATA-REP-008.businessDetail: Without clear search data, you can't see what products customers are looking for, whether they find them, or what search failures happen. You're missing insights into product demand, customer pain points, and opportunities to add new products. Your merchandising team can't use search data to improve navigation or highlight products that customers want but can't find.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-222-0-DATA-REP-008.businessFix:
1. Enable search tracking in your Shopify store settings.
2. Verify your analytics is configured to capture and report search terms.
3. Review your search report monthly to understand which products customers seek.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-222-0-DATA-REP-008.businessVerify: After the fix, you'll see a list of what customers search for, showing which products are in demand and which search queries return no results.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-224-0-DATA-REP-011.businessIssue: Your overall analytics setup is mostly correct but has 2 gaps that should be addressed to ensure complete tracking of all sales and customer activity.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-224-0-DATA-REP-011.businessDetail: The gaps are in cash-on-delivery payment tracking and login event tracking. If you accept cash-on-delivery payments or have registered customer accounts, these aren't being tracked properly. This means you can't see which payment methods work best or whether returning customers spend more than new customers. These insights are important for understanding your full customer journey and payment strategy.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-224-0-DATA-REP-011.businessFix:
1. Confirm whether cash-on-delivery tracking is needed for your store.
2. Determine if customer login tracking matters to your business.
3. Work with your developer to add tracking for these two event types.
4. Test both flows (complete a cash-on-delivery purchase, create an account and log in) to verify they appear in analytics.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-224-0-DATA-REP-011.businessVerify: After the fix, your analytics will capture all payment types and customer login events, giving you a complete view of the full purchase journey.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-225-0-DATA-REP-016.businessIssue: Your analytics is tracking 43,366 different page URLs when it should only have a few hundred — a huge number of page variations is fragmenting your data and hiding real performance.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-225-0-DATA-REP-016.businessDetail: This happens when the same product or page gets multiple URLs (with/without trailing slash, on different collection pages). Your reports show each variation separately, so you can't see total performance of a product across all ways it's reached. You can't identify bestsellers because their sales are split across multiple entries. Reports become useless because nothing shows up clearly.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-225-0-DATA-REP-016.businessFix:
1. Audit your Shopify store URL structure for duplicates and variations.
2. Set up URL canonicalization so your store uses clean, consistent URLs.
3. Configure your analytics to strip tracking parameters from page URLs.
4. Create redirect rules so all variations (with/without trailing slash) point to one canonical URL.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-225-0-DATA-REP-016.businessVerify: After the fix, your analytics will show 300-500 unique pages instead of 43,000, and product reports will consolidate all sales for each product into one line item.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-226-0-DATA-UTM-002.businessIssue: Your analytics shows Facebook traffic split between two labels ('fb' and 'facebook') instead of consolidated in one row — fragmenting Facebook's reported performance.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-226-0-DATA-UTM-002.businessDetail: Because the same traffic source is labeled two different ways, Facebook shows up as two separate channels in your reports, each looking smaller than it really is. You can't see total Facebook revenue, so you might think Facebook is underperforming when it's actually a top channel. You could mistakenly cut budget to one label while scaling the other, not realizing they're the same source.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-226-0-DATA-UTM-002.businessFix:
1. Audit all your Facebook ad campaigns and custom links to identify which are using 'fb' and which use 'facebook'.
2. Standardize on 'facebook' for all campaigns going forward.
3. Update any existing links or campaigns that use 'fb' to use 'facebook' instead.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-226-0-DATA-UTM-002.businessVerify: After the fix, all Facebook traffic will appear under one 'Facebook' label in your reports, showing the true total revenue from Facebook.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-227-0-DATA-UTM-003.businessIssue: The same Facebook tracking domain appears as both paid and referral traffic in your reports — the same source is showing up twice with different labels.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-227-0-DATA-UTM-003.businessDetail: Because the same traffic is split, you can't see true Facebook revenue. Part shows under 'paid' and part under 'referral,' splitting what should be one total. Your team might think Facebook has two separate channels, when it's actually one source tracked inconsistently. You can't accurately measure Facebook ROI or make scaling decisions.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-227-0-DATA-UTM-003.businessFix:
1. Add Facebook's tracking domain to your analytics referral exclusion list.
2. Ensure all Facebook links include proper tracking codes labeled as Facebook paid traffic.
3. Verify that Facebook clicks arriving without tracking are handled properly by your tracking setup.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-227-0-DATA-UTM-003.businessVerify: After the fix, Facebook traffic will consolidate into one 'Facebook' channel, showing complete revenue attribution without splits.

Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-228-0-DATA-UTM-004.businessIssue: Your mobile Facebook traffic is being counted as generic referral traffic instead of being labeled as Facebook paid traffic.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-228-0-DATA-UTM-004.businessDetail: Your analytics shows 22,454 sessions and EGP 606,086 in revenue from mobile Facebook ads, but they're hidden under 'referral' instead of 'Facebook.' This misattribution hides your mobile ad performance and makes organic referrals look better than they are. You can't measure mobile ad ROI or optimize mobile ad spending.
Client-Audit-GA4-GA4-228-0-DATA-UTM-004.businessFix:
1. Add mobile Facebook's domain to your analytics referral exclusion list.
2. Tag all mobile Facebook campaigns with proper tracking codes labeled as Facebook paid traffic.
